questions.prompt: A low-pass filter passes frequencies:
questions.explanation: LPF: passes f < f_c, attenuates f > f_c. HPF: opposite. BPF: passes a band between f_L and f_H.

questions.prompt: The cutoff (−3 dB) frequency of a simple RC low-pass filter is:
questions.explanation: f_c = 1/(2πRC) Hz (or ω_c = 1/RC rad/s). At f_c, |H| = 1/√2 ≈ −3 dB.

questions.prompt: The transfer function of a first-order passive RC low-pass filter is:
questions.explanation: RC LPF: H(s) = (1/sC)/(R + 1/sC) = 1/(1+sRC) = ω_c/(s+ω_c) where ω_c = 1/RC.

questions.prompt: A first-order high-pass RC filter rolls off at _____ below the cutoff frequency.
questions.explanation: First-order filter: −20 dB/decade (= −6 dB/octave) slope in the stopband. Second-order: −40 dB/decade.

questions.prompt: A Butterworth filter is characterized by:
choices: A. Maximally flat (monotonic) response in the passband with no ripple
C. Equiripple in both passband and stopband
questions.explanation: Butterworth: maximally flat magnitude—no ripple in pass or stopband, monotonically decreasing. Chebyshev: passband ripple for steeper rolloff.

questions.prompt: A Chebyshev Type I filter has:
choices: B. Equiripple in the passband and monotone stopband
C. Equiripple in both passband and stopband
questions.explanation: Chebyshev I: equiripple passband, monotone stopband. Steeper rolloff than Butterworth of same order.

questions.prompt: A Bessel filter is designed to maximize:
choices: C. Linear phase response (constant group delay)
questions.explanation: Bessel filter: maximally linear phase (constant group delay) → minimum signal distortion. Magnitude roll-off is less sharp than Butterworth.

questions.prompt: An Elliptic (Cauer) filter has:
choices: A. Equiripple in both passband AND stopband — sharpest rolloff for given order
questions.explanation: Elliptic: equiripple in both bands → sharpest transition. Tradeoff: complex design, nonlinear phase.

questions.prompt: The quality factor Q of a bandpass filter is:
choices: A. Q = f_0 / BW (center frequency / −3 dB bandwidth)
questions.explanation: Q = f_0/BW. High Q → narrow bandwidth, sharper selectivity. For series RLC: Q = ω₀L/R = 1/(ω₀RC).

questions.prompt: A Bode magnitude plot approximation uses _____ at a pole frequency.
choices: B. A −20 dB/decade slope break (asymptote changes from 0 to −20 dB/decade)
questions.explanation: Bode approx: at each real pole ω_p, the slope decreases by 20 dB/decade. At each zero, slope increases 20 dB/decade.

questions.prompt: An RC LPF has R = 1 kΩ and C = 10 nF. The −3 dB frequency in kHz is approximately:
An active filter uses an op-amp to:
choices: A. Provide gain and allow filters with no inductors
B. Replace both R and C
C. Only increase the cutoff frequency
questions.explanation: Active filters: op-amp provides gain (>1 possible) and eliminates need for bulky inductors, suitable for audio/instrumentation frequencies.

questions.prompt: The Sallen-Key topology is a popular second-order active filter using:
choices: B. One op-amp with two RC sections providing two poles
questions.explanation: Sallen-Key: single op-amp + two R, two C → two complex poles. Simple, well-understood second-order active filter.

questions.prompt: A notch (band-reject) filter has:
choices: A. Zero attenuation at all frequencies
B. A deep attenuation at the notch frequency, passing all others
C. Maximum gain at center frequency
questions.explanation: Notch filter: attenuates a specific frequency (e.g., 60 Hz hum rejection) while passing all other frequencies.

questions.prompt: Group delay of a filter is defined as:
choices: A. τ_g = −dφ/dω (negative derivative of phase with respect to frequency)
B. The time to reach steady state
questions.explanation: Group delay: τ_g(ω) = −dφ(ω)/dω. Constant group delay ↔ linear phase ↔ no waveform distortion.

questions.prompt: Which filter types exhibit equiripple behavior? (Select ALL that apply.)
questions.explanation: Chebyshev I: passband equiripple; Chebyshev II: stopband equiripple; Elliptic: both bands equiripple. Butterworth is maximally flat (no ripple).

questions.prompt: A second-order low-pass filter's rolloff rate is:
questions.explanation: Each pole contributes −20 dB/decade. Second-order (2 poles): −40 dB/decade asymptotic rolloff.

questions.prompt: Frequency transformation converts a LPF prototype to a HPF by substituting:
questions.explanation: LP-to-HP: s → ω_c/s. LP-to-BP: s → (s² + ω₀²)/(Bs) where B = bandwidth.

questions.prompt: A 3rd-order Butterworth LPF has how many poles?
questions.explanation: An nth-order filter has n poles. 3rd-order Butterworth: 3 poles, one real and two complex conjugate.

questions.prompt: The bandwidth of a series RLC bandpass filter is:
questions.explanation: Series RLC: BW = R/L rad/s; f_BW = R/(2πL) Hz. Also Q = ω₀L/R → BW = ω₀/Q.

questions.prompt: A series RLC circuit has R = 10 Ω, L = 1 mH, C = 10 nF. The center frequency f₀ in kHz is approximately:
The phase margin (PM) of a feedback system at the gain crossover frequency should be:
questions.explanation: Phase margin: angle above −180° at gain crossover. PM > 0° → stable; PM > 45° → good stability margin.

questions.prompt: An all-pass filter has:
choices: A. Flat magnitude response but frequency-dependent phase
B. Zero phase shift at all frequencies
C. Flat phase but frequency-dependent magnitude
D. No output at any frequency
questions.explanation: All-pass filter: |H(jω)| = 1 for all ω (constant gain), but phase varies. Used for phase correction/equalization.

questions.prompt: An inductor L in series with a capacitor C forms a resonant circuit with ω₀ = ?
questions.explanation: Resonant frequency: ω₀ = 1/√(LC). At resonance, X_L = X_C (inductive and capacitive reactances cancel).

questions.prompt: For a first-order RC high-pass filter, the magnitude response at very low frequency (ω -> 0) is:
questions.explanation: HPF transfer function H(jω)=jωRC/(1+jωRC). As ω->0, numerator goes to 0 so |H|->0.
